title: About .Astronomy
body: // about About .Astronomy A conference series that builds a dynamic community of scientists and educators, exploiting the potential of modern computing and the web in the era of data-driven astronomy. Rather than focusing on scientific questions, .Astronomy focuses on innovative use of the web to develop new research tools, and to communicate with a broad audience through online platforms and innovative engagement resources. What happens at .Astronomy? Through talks, tutorials, unconferences and hack days, participants: - Gain new coding or maker skills - Learn about the latest data services and tools - Learn how to communicate and collaborate more effectively using web platforms - Broaden their views on what a career in astronomy can look like The Format A typical .Astronomy event runs for 3-4 days: - Day 0 (optional): Tutorial day with introductory sessions on tools and technologies - Day 1 : Talks and lightning talks from participants - Day 2 : Hack Day, where participants collaborate on projects proposed at the meeting - Day 3 : Unconference sessions proposed and voted on by attendees History .Astronomy was founded by Robert Simpson in 2008 at Cardiff University. What began as a small gathering of astronomers interested in the web has grown into a community of over 300 alumni spanning research, education, outreach, and industry. The conference has been held across the globe: Cardiff, Leiden, Oxford, Heidelberg, Cambridge MA, Chicago, Sydney, Cape Town, Baltimore, Toronto, New York, and Madrid. Contact - Community: Join the .Astronomy Slack - GitHub: github.com/dotastro - Twitter: @dotastronomy - Foundation: foundation@dotastronomy.com - Brain Trust nominations: braintrust@dotastronomy.com --- Background images This site uses images from space telescopes as background images, changing each visit. title: Nominate Someone for .Astronomy
body: // community Nominate Someone Organising .Astronomy and making it a successful event relies a lot on our own experiences as organisers and word-of-mouth recommendations. To ensure we cast a wide net for future .Astronomy or hack day stars, we'd like to invite you to tell us who we should be inviting. Do you have a friend or colleague who would benefit from attending .Astronomy? Someone who has awesome things to contribute? A top speaker you saw at another event who would be great for the .Astronomy community? Tell us about them below and we'll reach out. title: Hacks - .Astronomy 3
body: Hacks from .Astronomy 3 .Astronomy 3 was held at New College, Oxford, 4–8 April 2011. Hacks Chromotone Creators: Jon Yardley, Chris North, Haley Gomez, Edward Gomez Exploring multiwavelength astronomy with sound. Each waveband has a pitch; the intensity/brightness in that waveband is represented by the volume of a note. When you scan a mouse over an image, Chromotone plays a chord with components corresponding to the intensity at each pixel. This .Astronomy 3 version aimed to make a lighter, more web-friendly version of the original Processing implementation, with added functionality. Source --- Additional hacks to be documented. title: Talks - .Astronomy 8, Oxford 2016
body: // talks Talks 20-22 June 2016, Pembroke College, Oxford Invited Talks - Sarah Kendrew (ESA): JWST and .Astronomy: How do we make JWST a 21st century telescope? - Bruno Merin (ESA): ESA Sky: an interactive all-sky viewer - Carl Ferkinhoff : Open Hardware in Astronomy - Ali Swanson (University of Oxford / Zooniverse): The Zooniverse Platform and Citizen Science - James Gilbert (@labjg): Instrumentation Hacking and Starbugs - Geert Barentsen (@GeertHub): Open Science and K2 - Adam Miller : (mentioned in programme) - Daina Bouquin : Library Archives in Astronomy - Tom Robitaille : (mentioned in programme) Day Zero Tutorials and Talks Contributors to the Day Zero programme: - Sarah Kendrew - Arna Karick - Demitri Muna - Scott Thomas - Lisa Ballard - Brian Cherinka - Philipp Plewa - Thomas Robitaille - Edward Gomez - Geert Barentsen Topics covered: programming languages and tools, GitHub, web hosting, Flask, D3, web scraping, Django, argparse. Pre-Dinner Talk - Aleks Scholz : WETI: The Wait for Extraterrestrial Intelligence! Talk Highlights Sarah Kendrew discussed making JWST data and tools accessible to the community, including existing tools like WebbPSF for point spread function modelling. Bruno Merin demonstrated ESA Sky, showing the tool's ability to serve whole-sky maps from multiple missions including GALEX, 2MASS, WISE, Herschel, and Planck, with in-app data plotting. James Gilbert made a compelling case for hardware hacking, describing how he proved that vacuum starbugs could work for fibre positioning in spectrographs in just a few days, when the formal process had stalled. Geert Barentsen argued for radical openness in data policy, using K2 as an example. K2's open data policy, with no proprietary period, had led to more unique authors and more early-career scientists engaging with the science compared to the original Kepler mission. title: .Astronomy 8 - Oxford, 2016
body: .Astronomy 8 Oxford, UK, 2016 📅 20–23 June 2016 📍 Pembroke College, Oxford University The eighth .Astronomy conference was held at Pembroke College, Oxford. It was organised by Becky Smethurst, an Oxford PhD student at the time, supported by Chris Lintott and Sarah Kendrew. This edition also featured a Day 0 tutorials day, following the success of its introduction at .Astronomy 7 in Sydney. Organisers - Becky Smethurst (Oxford, lead organiser) - Chris Lintott (Oxford) - Sarah Kendrew - Edward Gomez - Ruth Angus - Mark Richardson - Sandor Kruk - Ellen Schallig - Sam Vaughan Programme Talks covered open science in the Kepler missions, hardware hacking, citizen science, LSST data schools, the use of library archives in astronomy, and ESA Sky. Talk contributors: Adam Miller, Carl Ferkinhoff, Ali Swanson, Geert Barentsen, James Gilbert, Tom Robitaille, Sarah Kendrew, and Daina Bouquin. Pre-dinner talk: Aleks Scholz on WETI, the Wait for Extraterrestrial Intelligence. Day 0 tutorials covered: various programming languages and tools, GitHub, web hosting, Flask, D3, webscraping, Django, and argparse. Tutorial contributors included Sarah Kendrew, Arna Karick, Demitri Muna, Scott Thomas, Lisa Ballard, Brian Cherinka, Phillip Plewa, Thomas Robitaille, Edward Gomez, and Geert Barentsen. The Hack Day took place on Wednesday 22nd June 2016. - Full talks list - Participants list Hacks 20 hacks documented Hacks from this event include: .draft, StingRA, Are you still talking?, arxiv59, The ArXiv Mailer, AstroBadger, AstroGoldStars, bvi-sonification, Fakespeare, Galaxy Zoo Maze, GitNL, Hacks Collector, Markov Papers, Meta^4hack, Project Apollo Stories, SIMBAD the Bot, Spacebar, Stellar Autopsies Zooniverse, and more. title: Talks - .Astronomy 5, Cambridge MA 2013
body: // talks Talks 16-18 September 2013, Microsoft NERD Center, Cambridge, Massachusetts Invited Talks - Stuart Lynn (Zooniverse / Adler Planetarium): A Smarter Zooniverse - Curtis Wong (Microsoft Research): WorldWide Telescope - David Hogg (New York University): Crowdsourcing an All-Sky Survey - Alberto Accomazzi (Smithsonian/NASA ADS): The dotAstrophysics Data System (ADS 2.0) - Amanda Bauer (Australian Astronomical Observatory): Communication Strategies Short Talks / Lightning Talks - Elisabeth Newton (Harvard): Astrobites and astronomy blogging Talk Highlights Stuart Lynn opened the conference discussing the future of the Zooniverse, arguing that as Big Data grows, citizen science projects need smarter analysis methods and better use of volunteer time. David Hogg presented an audacious project: using astronomical images posted to Flickr by astrophotographers worldwide to build an all-sky survey. Using astrometry.net to calibrate the images, his team was beginning to find stars that the professional astronomical literature had never catalogued. Hogg argued that the "spam images" of the internet, properly calibrated, could reproduce the depth of professional surveys. Curtis Wong demonstrated WorldWide Telescope's capabilities for research, education, and multi-dimensional data exploration. Alberto Accomazzi announced the launch of ADS 2.0, featuring a new search engine with iterative refinement, a public API, and powerful query capabilities including grant acknowledgment searches and citation analysis excluding self-citations. The announcement that ADS was hiring got enthusiastic applause. Unconference Sessions - Speaking up for diversity and equality - AstroQuery: web tool for querying catalogs - Outreach in public settings and measuring impact - How to build a killer astronomy show with WWT - Massively Open Online Courses (MOOCs) - Share the Love: a better system for credit in astronomy - Connecting popular literature to the astro literature - The future of astronom

title: .Astronomy 5 - Cambridge MA, 2013
body: .Astronomy 5 Cambridge, MA, USA, 2013 📅 16–18 September 2013 📍 New England Research and Development Center, Cambridge, Massachusetts The fifth .Astronomy conference was held at Microsoft Research's New England Research and Development (NERD) Center in Cambridge, Massachusetts, hosted by the Seamless Astronomy group at Harvard's Center for Astrophysics. It was the first .Astronomy event in North America, bringing the conference to a new continent and a new community of astronomers, educators, and technologists. 60% of attendees were new to the meeting. The gender balance was 33% female, which Rob Simpson noted was "decent, but could be better." title: .Astronomy 4: Heidelberg, 2012
body: .Astronomy 4: Heidelberg, 2012 Date: 9-11 July 2012 Location: Haus der Astronomie (HdA) and Internationales Wissenschaftsforum Heidelberg (IWH), Heidelberg, Germany Theme: Visualisation, JavaScript/Python in the browser, crowdsourcing, career development, hacking the literature, global education Attendance: ~60 people Organisers: Sarah Kendrew, Thomas Robitaille (MPIA), Markus Possel (HdA) About The fourth .Astronomy was hosted by the Max Planck Institute for Astronomy in Heidelberg, using two venues: the recently completed Haus der Astronomie (a centre for public engagement) and the Internationales Wissenschaftsforum in Heidelberg's historic centre. Speakers included Prof Bruce Berriman (Caltech), Dr Tom Robitaille (MPIA), Dr Robert Simpson (Oxford), Dr Matthew Turk (Columbia), Dr Eli Bressert (Exeter/ESO), Geert Barentsen (Armagh Observatory), Dr Matthew Graham (Caltech), Prof David Hogg (NYU), Jonathan Fay (Microsoft Research), Dr Aleks Scholz (DIAS), Dr Robert Hollow (CSIRO), Julie Steele and Noah Iliinsky (O'Reilly Media), and Dr Arfon Smith (Adler Planetarium). The conference was summarised in an "Unproceedings" paper on the arXiv (arXiv:1301.5193). Supported by MPIA, the Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft (DFG), and the Haus der Astronomie. title: Talks - .Astronomy 4, Heidelberg 2012
body: // talks Talks 9-11 July 2012, Haus der Astronomie and IWH, Heidelberg Invited Talks - Kevin Govender (IAU Office for Astronomy Development): Astronomy for a Better World - Markus Poessel (Haus der Astronomie): Haus der Astronomie - Amit Kapadia (Adler Planetarium): AstroJS: Javascript for Astronomy - August Muench (Harvard CfA): Astronomy Dataverse - Thomas Robitaille (MPIA Heidelberg): Astro Python - Bruce Berriman (IPAC / Caltech): Astronomy in the Cloud - Thomas Kitching (University of Edinburgh): Astronomy Crowdsourcing - Michelle Borkin (Harvard): Mechanical Turk, Visualisation and 'Glue' - Julie Steele (O'Reilly Media) and Noah Iliinsky (Consultant/Author): How to be a Data Visualisation Star - Kelle Cruz (CUNY): AstroBetter - Alberto Pepe (Harvard CfA): Data Mining and ADS - Alyssa Goodman (Harvard CfA): Seamless Astronomy Talk Highlights Kevin Govender's talk on using astronomy for development was particularly well-received, with extensive audience discussion about the role of astronomy in society. The talk covered the IAU Office for Astronomy Development's mission to use astronomy as a tool for development, particularly in Africa and the developing world. Michelle Borkin demonstrated how Amazon's Mechanical Turk could be used for astronomical classification tasks, and showed the Glue visualisation tool for multi-dimensional data exploration. Julie Steele and Noah Iliinsky delivered an engaging session on data visualisation principles, drawing from their O'Reilly publications. Alyssa Goodman's Seamless Astronomy talk discussed linking data, literature, and visualisation tools, with live demonstrations of how different software could communicate via SAMP. title: .Astronomy α (DotDotAstro): Online, 2020
body: .Astronomy α (DotDotAstro): Online, 2020 Date: 17, 19, and 26 August 2020 Location: Online (synchronous sessions 14:00-21:00 UTC) Theme: Virtual conferencing, accessibility, global participation Attendance: ~75 people (max) Organisers: Sarah Kendrew (ESA/STScI), Chris Lintott (Oxford), Robert Simpson (Google), Becky Smethurst (Oxford) About The first online-only edition of .Astronomy, originally planned before the COVID-19 pandemic as an experiment in truly virtual conferencing. Styled as "..Astro" or ".Astronomy α", this event ran over three non-consecutive days in August 2020. The conference featured the usual mix of talks, unconferencing, and a hack day, adapted for the online format. Key talks were recorded and edited in advance to avoid the "shouting into the void" feeling of remote presentations. To preserve the small group feel of a normal .Astronomy, participation was capped at around 75. The event especially welcomed those who had been unable to attend in-person events due to travel constraints, family commitments, funding, or environmental concerns. The three non-consecutive days were an experiment to reduce barriers to participation by not requiring three straight days of availability. title: .Astronomy Alpha - Online, 2020
body: .Astronomy alpha Online, 2020 📅 17, 19, and 26 August 2020 📍 Online (synchronous parts ran 14:00–21:00 UTC) DotDotAstro (also known as .Astronomy alpha) was the first fully online edition of the .Astronomy conference. Amazingly, this had been planned before the COVID-19 pandemic as an experiment in what a truly online conference could be, though the pandemic made it all the more relevant. The conference ran over three non-consecutive days in August 2020 to reduce barriers to participation. It was open to a maximum of around 75 participants to preserve the small-group feel of a normal .Astronomy. The synchronous parts ran from 14:00–21:00 UTC to accommodate participants across time zones. The conference especially welcomed participation from those who had been unable to join in-person .Astronomy events in the past, whether due to family commitments, funding constraints, or reduced travel for environmental reasons. title: .Astronomy 6 - Chicago, 2014
body: .Astronomy 6 Chicago, IL, USA, 2014 📅 8–10 December 2014 📍 Adler Planetarium, Chicago, Illinois The sixth .Astronomy conference was held at the Adler Planetarium in Chicago, Illinois. The Adler had taken the lead among planetariums worldwide in establishing an astronomy and astrophysics research group and a citizen science department in a museum setting. Founded in 1930, it was the first modern planetarium in the Western Hemisphere. This was a particularly significant edition: it included visitors from non-astronomy fields, supported by the Alfred P. Sloan Foundation, to observe and potentially replicate the .Astronomy format in their own disciplines. The event also marked a strong upward trend in diversity, building on discussions from .Astronomy 5. Organisers - Laura Trouille - Lucianne Walkowicz - Laura Whyte Supported by - GitHub - The Alfred P. Sloan Foundation Programme Talks were given by Arfon Smith (GitHub for Science), Erin Braswell (Open Science Framework), Dustin Lang (Astrometry.net), Alberto Pepe (Authorea), Stefano Meschiari (Super Planet Crash), Laura Whyte (Engaging Young People), and Adam Becker (How to Destroy Science Publishing). Lightning talks featured contributions from Geert Barentsen, Emily Rice, Demitri Muna, Grace Wolf-Chase, Meredith Rawls, Angelle Tanner, Alice Allen, Alyssa Goodman, Chris Lintott, Vladimir Sudilovsky, and many more. title: .Astronomy 2: Leiden, 2009
body: .Astronomy 2: Leiden, 2009 Date: 30 November to 4 December 2009 Location: Lorentz Center, Leiden University, Netherlands Theme: Data-driven astronomy, citizen science, new media, visualisation Attendance: ~50 people Organisers: Information to be added: submit a PR! About The second .Astronomy conference was the first to follow the ".Astronomy format" with a hack day. Hosted at the Lorentz Center at Leiden University, it explored how new technologies were changing astronomy for researchers and the public alike. Key themes included citizen science (Galaxy Zoo), web-based platforms, new media for outreach (podcasting, blogging, microblogging), networked technologies for research (virtual observatory, literature tools, data mining), and visualisation (Google Sky, Microsoft WorldWide Telescope). Invited speakers included Dr Michael Nielsen (author of Reinventing Discovery), Dr Arfon Smith (Oxford University), Dr John Taylor (Google), and Prof Andy Lawrence (Edinburgh University). Sponsors included the Lorentz Center, Astron, the Royal Astronomical Society, the British Council, NWO, RadioNet, and Platform Beta Techniek. body: .Astronomy 2 Leiden, Netherlands, 2009 📅 30 November – 4 December 2009 📍 Lorentz Centre, Leiden University The second .Astronomy conference was hosted at the Lorentz Center, Leiden University. It was the first to follow the ".Astronomy format", including a Hack Day. The conference brought together researchers, educators and technologists to discuss how a new era of surveys, web technologies and citizen science was transforming astronomy. As the event description put it: "Astronomy is facing a paradigm shift. The huge quantities of data that will be generated by a new generation of surveys and instruments require new ways of thinking. At the same time, an ever more connected world is bringing astronomy to the masses by the vast possibilities of the web." title: .Astronomy 11 - Toronto, 2019
body: .Astronomy 11 Toronto, Canada, 2019 📅 22–25 October 2019 📍 Chestnut Residence and Conference Centre, 89 Chestnut St, Toronto, Ontario The 11th .Astronomy conference was hosted by the Dunlap Institute for Astronomy and Astrophysics at the University of Toronto. This was the first time .Astronomy came to Canada, a country with a rich heritage in astronomy, including the development of Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI), the discovery of Supernova 1987A, and observations of the first known black hole, Cygnus X-1. Toronto has a large community of astronomers with interests across both astronomy and software/data issues, making it an ideal host for .Astronomy 11. title: .Astronomy 7: Sydney, 2015
body: .Astronomy 7: Sydney, 2015 Date: 3-6 November 2015 Location: Justice & Police Museum, Sydney, Australia Theme: Open source in astronomy, education, cross-disciplinary research Attendance: ~60 people Organisers: Amanda Bauer, Arna Karick, James Allen, Vanessa Moss, Robert Hollow About .Astronomy 7 was the first .Astronomy conference location outside Europe and the United States, held at the Justice & Police Museum in Sydney. This event introduced ".Astronomy Day Zero", a day of introductory tutorials on commonly used tools during the Hack Day. This optional day aimed to help participants who felt they needed more skills to fully participate in the Hack Day. Featured speakers included Alice Williamson (University of Sydney) on Open Source Malaria, Tom Robitaille on Astropy and the open-source revolution in astronomy, Kirsten Gottschalk on astronomy education mini-websites, and Nuria Lorente (AAO) on software development from the perspectives of astronomers and engineers. Full speaker list: Tom Robitaille, Lisa Ballard, Alice Williamson, Danny Vohl, Yeshe Fenner, Nuria Lorente, Sze-leung Cheng, Kirsten Gottschalk. Sarah Kendrew gave a public lecture on Giant Telescopes of the Future at the University of Sydney during the conference. Contents - Agenda - Talks - Hacks - Unconference Sessions - Participants

title: .Astronomy 1 - Cardiff, 2008
body: .Astronomy 1 Cardiff, UK, 2008 📅 22–24 September 2008 📍 Cardiff University Department of Physics and Astronomy The first .Astronomy conference was held at Cardiff University in September 2008. It was organised solely by Robert Simpson, a Cardiff PhD student at the time, with support from Prof. Derek Ward-Thompson. The original subtitle was "Networked Astronomy and the New Media". It brought together approximately 30 astronomers, educators, and technologists to explore how the web and new media could transform astronomical research and outreach. The event featured a mix of live and remote presentations. Many attendees were converted to Twitter during the event. There was much discussion of social media and its potential for science. Formal proceedings were published by Canopus Publishing (edited by Robin Rees). It was something the organisers later noted was far too complicated and went out of date quickly. All subsequent events used the more informal "Unproceedings" format. Organisers - Robert Simpson (Cardiff University) - Derek Ward-Thompson (Cardiff University) Programme The event featured live talks on: - Blogging in astronomy - Hacking open data - Twitter for Telescopes - The Faulkes Telescope Project - WETI (The Wait for Extraterrestrial Intelligence) Remote Skype presentations were given by: - Phil Plait (Bad Astronomy blog), on blogging - Emily Lakdawalla (The Planetary Society), on public access to open imagery from the Solar System - Alberto Conti and Carol Christian (STScI), on Google Sky Hacks There was no Hack Day at the first .Astronomy event. GitHub Repos No GitHub repos linked to this event. Venue Room WX3.09, Queen's Buildings, Cardiff School of Physics and Astronomy, Cardiff University, The Parade, Cardiff CF24 3AA, UK. body: // talks Talks 22-24 September 2008, Cardiff University Speakers The first .Astronomy featured a mix of live and remote presentations. Topics ranged across blogging, citizen science, open data, and the emerging social web in astronomy. Live Talks - Joshua Bloom (UC Berkeley): PAIRITEL and robotic telescope networks - Will Gater (Sky at Night Magazine): Astronomy journalism and new media - Pamela Gay (Astronomy Cast / Star Stryder): Podcasting astronomy - Edward Gomez (Las Cumbres Observatory / Cardiff University) - Andy Lawrence (Royal Observatory Edinburgh / AstroGRID) - Chris Lintott (Galaxy Zoo / The Sky at Night): Galaxy Zoo and citizen science - Stuart Lowe (The Jodcast / Astronomy Blog) - Nick Rattenbury (The Jodcast) - Ian Robson (IYA 2009): The International Year of Astronomy - Paul Roche (Faulkes Telescope Project): The Faulkes Telescope Project Remote / Skype Presentations - Phil Plait (Bad Astronomy): Astronomy blogging - Emily Lakdawalla (The Planetary Society): Public access to open imagery from the Solar System - Carol Christian and Alberto Conti (STScI): Google Sky Trending Topics Skype, Twitter, Google Sky, Hacking, Galaxy Zoo Proceedings .Astronomy 1 had formal proceedings published by Canopus Publishing, edited by Robin Rees. This was the only .Astronomy to have traditional proceedings; the format was later replaced by the more informal "Unproceedings". title: .Astronomy 1: Cardiff, 2008
body: .Astronomy 1: Cardiff, 2008 Date: September 2008 Location: Cardiff University, Astronomy and Physics Department, Cardiff, UK Theme: Astronomy and the web: blogging, open data, citizen engagement Attendance: ~30 people Organisers: Robert Simpson About The very first .Astronomy event, organised solely by Robert Simpson, then a PhD student at Cardiff University. This was the conference that started it all, bringing together astronomers interested in how the web could transform their science and outreach. The event featured talks about blogging, hacking open data, Twitter for Telescopes, The Faulkes Telescope Project, and WETI (The Wait for Extraterrestrial Intelligence). Remote Skype presentations were given by Phil Plait (the Bad Astronomer) on blogging, Emily Lakdawalla (The Planetary Society) on public access to Solar System imagery, and Alberto Conti and Carol Christian (STScI) on Google Sky. Formal proceedings were published by Canopus, though this approach was later abandoned as too complex and quickly out of date. There was no Hack Day at this first event. title: Community Repos
body: .Astronomy Community Repositories GitHub repositories created by .Astronomy participants, many originating as conference hacks. dotastro/hacks-collector dotAstronomy Hacks Collector - the community database of conference hacks, with 12 stars. ESA-Datalabs/dotAstronomy13 .Astronomy 13 hackathon deposit - resources and hacks from the 2023 Madrid event. jegpeek/dynabooks A dynamic notebook hack - tools for interactive astronomical notebooks. barentsen/dotastro-argparse-tutorial Python argparse tutorial from .Astronomy 8 - a hands-on guide to command-line argument parsing. slowe/dotAstroPlayer Web interface for .Astronomy conference talks - a browser-based talk player. astrofrog/robo-ph A dotastro hack - robotic proposal helper built during a conference hack day. stefano-meschiari/dotastro-game Game template hack - a starting point for browser-based astronomy games from a .Astronomy hack day. dfm/ArXivMailer ArXiv Mailer hack from .Astronomy 2016 - a tool for personalised arXiv paper digests. vcalderon2009/dotAstronomy X .Astronomy X resources - materials and hacks from the tenth conference in Baltimore. bmorris3/dotastro9 astroplan astroquery Tutorials from .Astronomy 9 - astroplan and astroquery workshop materials from Cape Town 2017.

title: The .Astronomy Foundation
body: // foundation The .Astronomy Foundation Created to support not only the projects and collaborations that emerge from the .Astronomy conference series, but any projects undertaken in that spirit. Our Purpose "For the public benefit, the advancement of astronomy and related fields, in any part of the world, by people using technology to innovate the practice and communication of the subject, by facilitating and supporting work that has the potential to be transformative, interdisciplinary, or uniquely impactful, and the communication of such work in suitable venues." From our founding Charitable Trust Deed, April 2019. What We Support .Astronomy began as a way to encourage and facilitate new ideas in astronomy. We have always strived to be an open and inclusive environment for everyone to participate in the best subject in the universe. We aim to encourage the innovative use of technology to further the practice and communication of astronomy around the world. If you have a project that fits this mission, we want to hear from you.
